text: Hello, I'm sharing another robotic sigmoid. This was a patient who clinically presented with a diverticular perforation and a small abscess between the rectum and the vagina. She essentially after that time treated with antibiotics had smoldering disease and was really never asymptomatic. was not able to get a colonoscopy because of the concerns of the active disease and had never had a colonoscopy prior. Additionally, on imaging on more than one CAT scan, there was concern of a mass in the sigmoid colon. So even though clinically this all sounded like diverticulitis, I did the case as if it was a cancer.

The surgeon carefully dissects omental adhesions from previous abdominal operations, revealing the transverse colon. The transverse colon is splayed upwards to elevate the small bowel out of the pelvis. A sponge is used to keep the small bowel elevated. The surgeon begins medial collateral dissection over the sacral promontory.
The surgeon identifies the retrorectal space and locates the ureter. The inferior mesenteric artery is targeted next, with the confluence of the left colic and superior hemorrhoidal arteries visible.

The surgeon creates a window behind the inferior mesenteric artery, proximal to the left colic, with visible bleeding managed by packing with a sponge. The ureter is identified, aided by the use of ICG for enhanced visualization.

The surgeon completes lateral mobilization towards the splenic flexure, revealing adhesions from a prior perforation near the bladder without any fistula. The surgical field progresses towards the pelvis, approaching the uterus.
The surgeon has suspended the uterus using a stitch, visible in the surgical field, to facilitate dissection. The residual abscess cavity is also visible in the operative area.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to assist in lateral mobilization, peeling the left adnexa off the colon. The instrument serves as a strong retractor and blunt dissector, effectively clearing smoke and debris. The procedure continues with dissection on the right-hand side, following the path of least resistance.

The surgeon has completely freed the specimen and proceeds to take the rectal mesentery. Due to the specimen's size, it cannot be extracted transrectally in one piece, necessitating an intracorporeal anastomosis and a separate extraction incision. The surgeon checks the length, confirming it is adequate, and takes the distal and proximal mesentery, ensuring to collect a substantial lymph node packet by coming underneath the transected inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon amputates a large epiploic appendage from the bowel to improve visibility. The proximal margin of the bowel is then divided using a stapler, with the Firefly imaging system being checked for speed.

The surgeon divides the distal bowel segment and prepares for a two-layer anastomosis, closing the ends of the specimen due to concerns of malignancy. The anastomosis technique involves stapled ends, ensuring a secure connection with a strong posterior row.

The surgeon performs a collotomy and proctotomy, using ICG to check for ischemia in the bowel tissue between the staple line and the incision. The tissue appears healthy, and the surgeon continues with the anastomosis, ensuring the posterior row is secure.
The surgeon begins suturing in the posterior midline of the anastomosis, progressing towards the right side, following the staple line.

The surgeon prepares to extract the specimen through a flint and steel incision, utilizing a previous incision from prior open surgery. The dissection is visually challenging due to the patient's body habitus and scarring in the area.

The surgeon carefully unties an inadvertently tied knot and proceeds to imbricate the front row of the anastomosis, ensuring the mucosa is tucked inward for optimal serosa-to-serosa opposition. The mucosa is smoothed out to prevent bunching during closure, with plans to sew an additional layer for reinforcement.
The surgeon ties two sutures together and retrieves the original suture to sew it over the top, forming an outer anterior row in the two-layer anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis using a rigid proctoscope, observing no bubbles and confirming the anastomosis is widely patent with no bleeding. The fluid is removed from the surgical site, and no drains are left in place.
